lessonTitle: Tuition, Living Costs, and Total Cost of Attendance
concept: Each of the six TCA categories behaves differently — knowing which ones you can control is the first step to managing them.

hook.fact: €600 rent. €300 food. €100 transport. €600 materials. €200 personal. Add tuition: €12,000.
hook.question: Which number is actually within your control?

conceptCards.title: Card 1
conceptCards.body: Tuition is set by your university and your programme. Engineering typically costs more than humanities at the same institution. International students almost always pay more than domestic students.

conceptCards.title: Card 2
conceptCards.body: Accommodation is usually your second-biggest cost. On-campus dormitories are convenient. Off-campus shared flats are often cheaper but require deposits, utility bills, and more planning.

conceptCards.title: Card 3
conceptCards.body: Food costs vary enormously by approach. University meal plans are predictable but expensive per meal. Self-catering is cheaper but requires time. Most students end up doing both — and overspend on the unplanned bits.

conceptCards.title: Card 4
conceptCards.body: Transport has three layers: daily commuting, holiday travel home, and spontaneous local movement. If you are studying abroad, factor in at least one or two international flights per year.

conceptCards.title: Card 5
conceptCards.body: Course materials are easy to underestimate. New textbooks can cost $50–$200 each. Always check whether your university provides free software licences before buying anything. Secondhand copies are often available at a fraction of the cost.

conceptCards.title: Card 6
conceptCards.body: Now test this: Personal spending is the most underestimated category because no single purchase feels significant. Phone, clothing, toiletries, gym, subscriptions, socialising — budget $100–$300/month as a floor, more in expensive cities.

questions.prompt: Which TCA category is almost entirely outside your control?
questions.explanation: "Tuition is set by the institution — unlike food, transport, and personal spending which all involve choices you control."

questions.statement: On-campus housing is always cheaper than renting off-campus.
questions.whyPrompt: Why is that true or false?
questions.explanation: "Off-campus shared housing is often cheaper per room — though it adds complexity like utility bills, deposits, and a longer commute."

questions.prompt: Rank these TCA categories from typically most to least expensive for an international student:
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.prompt: Match each cost type to its controllability:

pairs.right: Partially controllable — choice of on/off campus
Highly controllable — meal plan vs self-catering vs mixed

questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.sentence: "International students almost always pay ___ tuition than domestic students at the same university."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.prompt: Kai buys textbooks new for 4 courses at an average of $80 each. He could buy secondhand at 40% of the price.

questions.sentence: Complete the sentence: "Choosing between a meal plan and self-catering is not only about ___ — it also depends on how much ___ you have and how reliably you can ___."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.scenario: Kai's monthly budget breakdown: Tuition (monthly): €1,000 | Accommodation: €700 | Food: €150 | Transport: €20 | Materials: €20 | Personal: €50 | Total: €1,940
options: Food and personal spending are almost certainly underestimated for any major city
questions.explanation: "€150/month for food is extremely tight — most students spend €250–€400. €50 personal spending in a city is unrealistic. These two categories will almost certainly overshoot."

tasks.scenario: Build Priya's annual TCA for a Dutch university.
tasks.explanation: "Priya's real annual cost is more than double her tuition figure. This is typical — non-tuition costs often match or exceed tuition at mid-fee EU universities."

tasks.prompt: Which option makes more financial and practical sense for Marcus?
tasks.explanation: "At 15 hours/week work plus full-time study, Marcus has limited spare time. The €1,600 saving is real, but the 45-minute daily cooking commitment adds up to over 270 hours/year — worth calculating against his hourly earnings."

lockIn.takeaway: Tuition is fixed. Almost every other TCA category has real choices attached — and those choices compound across four years.

reviewQueueEntry.definition: Cost categories anchors better education money decisions.
reviewQueueEntry.quickRecallPrompt: What does Cost categories include beyond tuition?
